 {"id":"bd-jfkk","title":"Audit and enhance ingest loop to provide all status metadata without Lua","description":"## Overview

Verify that wa's existing ingest/polling loop already provides all the metadata that STATUS_UPDATE_LUA was sending, and enhance it if needed. The goal is to confirm we lose nothing by removing the Lua hook.

## Current STATUS_UPDATE_LUA Payload

The Lua snippet sends this JSON to \`wa event --from-status\`:
\`\`\`json
{
  "schema_version": 0,
  "pane_id": 123,
  "domain": "local",
  "title": "zsh",
  "cursor": {"row": 10, "col": 5},
  "dimensions": {"rows": 24, "cols": 80},
  "is_alt_screen": false,
  "ts": 1706123456789
}
\`\`\`

## What Ingest Loop Already Provides

wa's ingest loop (crates/wa-core/src/ingest.rs) polls \`wezterm cli list --format json\`:

\`\`\`json
[
  {
    "window_id": 0,
    "tab_id": 0,
    "pane_id": 0,
    "workspace": "default",
    "size": "80x24",        // ✅ dimensions available
    "title": "zsh",         // ✅ title available  
    "cwd": "/home/user",    // ✅ cwd available (bonus!)
    "domain": "local",      // ✅ domain available (new in WezTerm)
    "is_active": true       // ✅ active state available
  }
]
\`\`\`

## Gap Analysis

| Field | Lua Provides | CLI Provides | Action Needed |
|-------|-------------|--------------|---------------|
| pane_id | ✅ | ✅ | None |
| domain | ✅ | ✅ | None |
| title | ✅ | ✅ | None |
| dimensions | ✅ (rows/cols) | ✅ (80x24 string) | Parse "80x24" → (80, 24) |
| cursor position | ✅ | ❌ | See notes below |
| is_alt_screen | ✅ | ❌ | Handled by bd-2v4t (escape sequence detection) |
| cwd | ❌ | ✅ | Bonus - we get MORE data |

### Cursor Position

The cursor position was rarely used by wa. Options:
1. **Remove dependency**: Confirm no wa code actually needs cursor position
2. **On-demand query**: If ever needed, \`wezterm cli get-text --pane-id X\` includes cursor info
3. **Escape sequence parsing**: Could track cursor from CSI sequences (complex, probably overkill)

**Recommendation**: Audit wa codebase to confirm cursor position is unused, document as unavailable in polling mode.

## Implementation Tasks

### 1. Parse Dimensions String
\`\`\`rust
fn parse_dimensions(size: &str) -> Option<(u16, u16)> {
    let parts: Vec<&str> = size.split('x').collect();
    if parts.len() == 2 {
        let cols = parts[0].parse().ok()?;
        let rows = parts[1].parse().ok()?;
        Some((cols, rows))
    } else {
        None
    }
}
\`\`\`

### 2. Verify Domain Field Available
Check WezTerm version requirements — \`domain\` field was added in WezTerm 20230712+.
Add fallback for older versions (default to "local").

### 3. Audit Cursor Usage
Search codebase for any dependency on cursor position:
\`\`\`bash
rg "cursor" crates/wa-core/src/
\`\`\`

### 4. Update PaneInfo Struct
Ensure ingest.rs PaneInfo struct includes all fields:
\`\`\`rust
pub struct PaneInfo {
    pub pane_id: u64,
    pub title: String,
    pub domain: String,
    pub cwd: Option<String>,
    pub dimensions: (u16, u16),  // (cols, rows)
    pub is_active: bool,
    pub is_alt_screen: bool,  // From ScreenStateTracker
}
\`\`\`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All STATUS_UPDATE_LUA fields available without Lua (except cursor if unused)
- [ ] Dimensions parsed from "WxH" string format
- [ ] Domain field handled with fallback for old WezTerm
- [ ] Cursor position usage audited and documented
- [ ] PaneInfo struct updated
- [ ] Unit tests for dimension parsing
- [ ] Integration test: ingest loop provides complete metadata

## Dependencies

- Depends on: bd-2v4t (alt-screen detection) for is_alt_screen field

## Files to Modify

- crates/wa-core/src/ingest.rs — PaneInfo struct and parsing
- crates/wa-core/src/wezterm.rs — CLI output parsing

## References

- wezterm cli list: https://wezfurlong.org/wezterm/cli/cli/list.html

### Findings:

**1. Cursor Position** ✅
- `PaneInfo` struct already has `cursor_x: Option<u32>` and `cursor_y: Option<u32>`
- Populated by `wezterm cli list --format json`
- Test at wezterm.rs:1284-1285 confirms parsing works

**2. Dimensions** ✅
- `PaneInfo.size` has rows/cols (nested struct)
- Fallback to `PaneInfo.rows`/`PaneInfo.cols` (flat fields)
- `effective_rows()`/`effective_cols()` methods provide unified access

**3. Alt-Screen** ✅ (escape sequence detection ALREADY EXISTS)
- `PaneCursor.in_alt_screen` is updated during `capture_snapshot()`
- Uses `detect_alt_screen_changes()` at ingest.rs:1573 to find ESC[?1049h/l sequences
- When Lua removed: use `cursor.in_alt_screen` as canonical source

**4. Domain, Title, CWD** ✅
- `PaneInfo.domain_name`, `PaneInfo.title`, `PaneInfo.cwd` all populated from CLI
- `effective_domain()`, `effective_title()`, `parsed_cwd()` helper methods exist

### Action Needed:
None for data availability. The next task (bd-30gj) can safely remove STATUS_UPDATE_LUA since all metadata is already available via CLI + escape sequence detection.

### Note on Two Alt-Screen Sources:
Currently IPC returns both:
- `alt_screen`: From PaneEntry.is_alt_screen (Lua IPC source - will become stale)
- `cursor_alt_screen`: From PaneCursor.in_alt_screen (escape sequence detection)

-{"id":"bd-mwst","title":"Document Lua removal: CHANGELOG, migration guide, architecture update","description":"## Overview

Update all documentation to reflect the removal of STATUS_UPDATE_LUA and the new polling-based architecture. This ensures users understand the change and can migrate smoothly.

## Documentation Updates Required

### 1. CHANGELOG.md

Add entry for the version that removes STATUS_UPDATE_LUA:

\`\`\`markdown
## [0.2.0] - 2026-XX-XX

### Changed

- **BREAKING**: Removed Lua-based status update hook (\`update-status\`)
  - Dramatically improves WezTerm performance by eliminating high-frequency Lua callbacks
  - Pane metadata now obtained via polling (\`wezterm cli list\`)
  - Alt-screen detection now via escape sequence parsing (more reliable)

### Removed

- \`wa event --from-status\` CLI command (internal, not public API)
- STATUS_UPDATE_LUA snippet from \`wa setup\` output

### Migration

If you previously ran \`wa setup\`, run it again to update your wezterm.lua:

\`\`\`bash
wa setup --wezterm
\`\`\`

This will automatically remove the deprecated Lua code from the WA-managed block.
\`\`\`

### 2. README.md / AGENTS.md

Update any references to status updates:

- Remove mentions of "real-time status updates"
- Update architecture diagram if it shows Lua→wa flow
- Add note about polling-based pane metadata

### 3. PLAN.md / PLAN_CODEX.md

Update the specification:

**Section to update:**
- §2.7 (Status update specification) — mark as deprecated/removed
- Appendix E.1 — remove STATUS_UPDATE_LUA documentation

**Add new section:**
- §X.X Polling-based pane metadata
- Explain how alt-screen detection works via escape sequences

### 4. Architecture Documentation

Create or update architecture docs explaining:

1. **Before (Lua-based)**:
   \`\`\`
   WezTerm update-status event (60Hz)
     → Lua handler (runs every event)
       → Rate limit check
         → JSON serialization
           → wezterm.background_child_process
             → wa event --from-status
               → IPC to watcher
   \`\`\`

2. **After (Polling-based)**:
   \`\`\`
   wa watcher ingest loop (configurable interval)
     → wezterm cli list --format json
       → Parse pane metadata
     → ScreenStateTracker.process_output()
       → Parse alt-screen escape sequences
   \`\`\`

### 5. Inline Code Documentation

Update doc comments in affected files:

- crates/wa-core/src/setup.rs: Update module-level docs
- crates/wa-core/src/ingest.rs: Document polling-based metadata
- crates/wa-core/src/screen_state.rs: Document escape sequence detection

### 6. Migration Guide

Create a dedicated migration section (can be in README or separate file):

\`\`\`markdown
## Migrating from wa 0.1.x to 0.2.x

### What Changed

In 0.2.0, we removed the Lua-based status update hook to dramatically improve WezTerm performance.

### Impact

- **No action required** for most users
- If you have custom integrations with \`wa event --from-status\`, see below

### Steps to Upgrade

1. **Update wa** to the latest version
2. **Re-run setup** to update your wezterm.lua:
   \`\`\`bash
   wa setup --wezterm
   \`\`\`
3. **Restart WezTerm** to pick up config changes

### Verifying the Migration

After upgrading, your wezterm.lua's WA-managed block should NOT contain:
- \`wezterm.on('update-status'\`
- \`wa_last_status_update\`
- \`WA_STATUS_UPDATE_INTERVAL_MS\`

It SHOULD still contain:
- \`wezterm.on('user-var-changed'\` (for agent signaling)

### Breaking Changes

- \`wa event --from-status\` command removed
- StatusUpdate IPC message type removed
- These were internal implementation details, not public APIs
\`\`\`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] CHANGELOG.md updated with breaking change notice
- [ ] README.md updated (if applicable)
- [ ] AGENTS.md updated (if applicable)
- [ ] PLAN.md §2.7 marked as deprecated
- [ ] Architecture documentation updated
- [ ] Migration guide written
- [ ] Code doc comments updated
- [ ] All docs spell-checked and links verified

## Dependencies

- Depends on: bd-o2h4 (E2E tests passing, feature complete)

## Files to Modify/Create

- CHANGELOG.md
- README.md (if applicable)
- AGENTS.md (if applicable)
- PLAN.md
- PLAN_CODEX.md
- docs/migration-0.2.md (new, optional)
- crates/wa-core/src/setup.rs (doc comments)
- crates/wa-core/src/ingest.rs (doc comments)

## References

 {"id":"bd-o2h4","title":"Update E2E tests: remove status_update scenario, add alt-screen detection test","description":"## Overview

Update the E2E test suite to reflect the removal of STATUS_UPDATE_LUA and addition of alt-screen detection via escape sequences.

## Current E2E Test State

The E2E test harness (scripts/e2e_test.sh) has a "status_update" scenario that:
1. Writes a minimal wezterm.lua with STATUS_UPDATE_LUA
2. Spawns a pane
3. Sends a status update via \`wa event --from-status\`
4. Verifies the watcher receives it

This scenario will become invalid and must be removed.

## Changes Required

### 1. Remove status_update Scenario

In SCENARIO_REGISTRY (~line 340):
\`\`\`bash
# REMOVE this entry:
"status_update:Validate status update forwarding lane (wezterm.lua -> wa event -> watcher)"
\`\`\`

Remove the entire \`run_scenario_status_update()\` function (~lines 2550-2650).

### 2. Add alt-screen Detection Scenario

New scenario: "alt_screen_detection"

Purpose: Verify wa detects alt-screen transitions via escape sequence parsing (not Lua).

Test flow:
\`\`\`bash
run_scenario_alt_screen_detection() {
    # 1. Start wa watch
    # 2. Spawn a pane
    # 3. Send ESC[?1049h (enter alt-screen) to pane
    # 4. Verify wa detects alt-screen = true
    # 5. Send ESC[?1049l (leave alt-screen)
    # 6. Verify wa detects alt-screen = false
}
\`\`\`

Implementation sketch:
\`\`\`bash
run_scenario_alt_screen_detection() {
    local scenario_dir="$1"
    local wait_timeout="${TEST_WAIT_TIMEOUT:-30}"
    
    log_step "Starting wa watch..."
    wa watch --foreground > "$scenario_dir/wa_watch.log" 2>&1 &
    local wa_pid=$!
    sleep 2
    
    log_step "Spawning test pane..."
    local pane_id=$(wezterm cli spawn --cwd /tmp -- bash)
    sleep 1
    
    log_step "Verifying initial state (not alt-screen)..."
    local state=$(wa robot state --format json)
    local is_alt=$(echo "$state" | jq -r ".data.panes[] | select(.pane_id == $pane_id) | .is_alt_screen")
    if [[ "$is_alt" != "false" ]]; then
        log_fail "Expected is_alt_screen=false initially, got: $is_alt"
        return 1
    fi
    
    log_step "Sending alt-screen enter sequence..."
    # ESC [ ? 1049 h
    printf '\e[?1049h' | wezterm cli send-text --pane-id "$pane_id" --no-paste
    sleep 0.5  # Allow detection
    
    log_step "Verifying alt-screen detected..."
    state=$(wa robot state --format json)
    is_alt=$(echo "$state" | jq -r ".data.panes[] | select(.pane_id == $pane_id) | .is_alt_screen")
    if [[ "$is_alt" != "true" ]]; then
        log_fail "Expected is_alt_screen=true after ESC[?1049h, got: $is_alt"
        return 1
    fi
    
    log_step "Sending alt-screen leave sequence..."
    # ESC [ ? 1049 l
    printf '\e[?1049l' | wezterm cli send-text --pane-id "$pane_id" --no-paste
    sleep 0.5
    
    log_step "Verifying alt-screen exited..."
    state=$(wa robot state --format json)
    is_alt=$(echo "$state" | jq -r ".data.panes[] | select(.pane_id == $pane_id) | .is_alt_screen")
    if [[ "$is_alt" != "false" ]]; then
        log_fail "Expected is_alt_screen=false after ESC[?1049l, got: $is_alt"
        return 1
    fi
    
    log_pass "Alt-screen detection via escape sequences works!"
    cleanup_pane "$pane_id"
    kill "$wa_pid" 2>/dev/null
}
\`\`\`

### 3. Update uservar_forwarding Scenario

Ensure the uservar_forwarding scenario still works (it should, we're only removing status updates).

Verify it doesn't depend on status update functionality.

### 4. Add Regression Test: No Lua Status Hook

New scenario: "no_lua_status_hook"

Purpose: Verify wezterm.lua doesn't contain STATUS_UPDATE_LUA after setup.

\`\`\`bash
run_scenario_no_lua_status_hook() {
    # 1. Run wa setup
    # 2. Read wezterm.lua
    # 3. Verify it contains USERVAR_FORWARDING_LUA
    # 4. Verify it does NOT contain 'update-status'
    # 5. Verify it does NOT contain 'wa_last_status_update'
}
\`\`\`

## Test Fixtures

Create/update test fixtures:

### New: tests/e2e/alt_screen_enter.txt
\`\`\`
ESC[?1049h
\`\`\`

### New: tests/e2e/alt_screen_leave.txt
\`\`\`
ESC[?1049l
\`\`\`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] status_update scenario removed from SCENARIO_REGISTRY
- [ ] run_scenario_status_update() function removed
- [ ] alt_screen_detection scenario added and passing
- [ ] no_lua_status_hook scenario added and passing
- [ ] uservar_forwarding scenario still passes
- [ ] All other E2E tests unaffected
- [ ] CI passes

## Dependencies

- Depends on: bd-2z6d (CLI handling removed)
- Depends on: bd-2v4t (alt-screen detection implemented)

## Files to Modify

- scripts/e2e_test.sh:
  - Remove status_update from SCENARIO_REGISTRY
  - Remove run_scenario_status_update()
  - Add alt_screen_detection scenario
  - Add no_lua_status_hook scenario

## References

Implement the wa-side Unix socket listener that receives events from vendored WezTerm's native integration. This replaces the Lua→CLI→IPC path with direct socket communication.

## Prerequisites

- Completed: bd-3gon (Protocol design)

## Implementation

### 1. Event Listener Module

Create \`crates/wa-core/src/native_events.rs\`:

\`\`\`rust
use std::path::PathBuf;
use tokio::net::{UnixListener, UnixStream};
use tokio::sync::broadcast;

/// Events received from native WezTerm integration.
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub enum NativeEvent {
    PaneOutput {
        pane_id: u64,
        data: Vec<u8>,
        timestamp: u64,
    },
    StateChange {
        pane_id: u64,
        title: String,
        rows: u16,
        cols: u16,
        is_alt_screen: bool,
        timestamp: u64,
    },
    UserVarChanged {
        pane_id: u64,
        name: String,
        value: String,
        timestamp: u64,
    },
    PaneCreated {
        pane_id: u64,
        domain: String,
        cwd: Option<String>,
        timestamp: u64,
    },
    PaneDestroyed {
        pane_id: u64,
        timestamp: u64,
    },
}

/// Listener for native WezTerm events.
pub struct NativeEventListener {
    socket_path: PathBuf,
    listener: UnixListener,
    event_tx: broadcast::Sender<NativeEvent>,
}

impl NativeEventListener {
    /// Create and bind the event listener socket.
    pub async fn bind(socket_path: PathBuf) -> Result<Self, Error> {
        // Remove stale socket if exists
        let _ = std::fs::remove_file(&socket_path);
        
        // Create parent directory
        if let Some(parent) = socket_path.parent() {
            std::fs::create_dir_all(parent)?;
        }
        
        let listener = UnixListener::bind(&socket_path)?;
        let (event_tx, _) = broadcast::channel(1024);
        
        Ok(Self {
            socket_path,
            listener,
            event_tx,
        })
    }
    
    /// Subscribe to events.
    pub fn subscribe(&self) -> broadcast::Receiver<NativeEvent> {
        self.event_tx.subscribe()
    }
    
    /// Run the event listener loop.
    pub async fn run(&self) -> Result<(), Error> {
        loop {
            let (stream, _) = self.listener.accept().await?;
            let event_tx = self.event_tx.clone();
            
            tokio::spawn(async move {
                if let Err(e) = handle_connection(stream, event_tx).await {
                    tracing::warn!("Native event connection error: {}", e);
                }
            });
        }
    }
}

async fn handle_connection(
    stream: UnixStream,
    event_tx: broadcast::Sender<NativeEvent>,
) -> Result<(), Error> {
    use tokio::io::{AsyncBufReadExt, BufReader};
    
    let reader = BufReader::new(stream);
    let mut lines = reader.lines();
    
    while let Some(line) = lines.next_line().await? {
        match parse_event(&line) {
            Ok(event) => {
                let _ = event_tx.send(event);  // Ignore if no subscribers
            }
            Err(e) => {
                tracing::debug!("Failed to parse native event: {}", e);
            }
        }
    }
    
    Ok(())
}

fn parse_event(json: &str) -> Result<NativeEvent, Error> {
    let value: serde_json::Value = serde_json::from_str(json)?;
    
    match value.get("type").and_then(|t| t.as_str()) {
        Some("pane_output") => Ok(NativeEvent::PaneOutput {
            pane_id: value["pane_id"].as_u64().unwrap_or(0),
            data: base64::decode(value["data"].as_str().unwrap_or(""))?,
            timestamp: value["ts"].as_u64().unwrap_or(0),
        }),
        Some("state_change") => Ok(NativeEvent::StateChange {
            pane_id: value["pane_id"].as_u64().unwrap_or(0),
            title: value["state"]["title"].as_str().unwrap_or("").to_string(),
            rows: value["state"]["rows"].as_u64().unwrap_or(24) as u16,
            cols: value["state"]["cols"].as_u64().unwrap_or(80) as u16,
            is_alt_screen: value["state"]["is_alt_screen"].as_bool().unwrap_or(false),
            timestamp: value["ts"].as_u64().unwrap_or(0),
        }),
        // ... other event types
        _ => Err(Error::UnknownEventType),
    }
}

### 2. Integration with Watcher

Modify \`crates/wa-core/src/watcher.rs\` or equivalent:

\`\`\`rust
impl Watcher {
    pub async fn run_with_native_events(&mut self) -> Result<()> {
        let native_listener = NativeEventListener::bind(
            self.config.native_event_socket.clone()
        ).await?;
        
        let mut native_rx = native_listener.subscribe();
        
        // Spawn listener task
        let listener_handle = tokio::spawn(async move {
            native_listener.run().await
        });
        
        loop {
            tokio::select! {
                // Existing poll loop
                _ = self.poll_interval.tick() => {
                    self.poll_panes().await?;
                }
                
                // Native events
                Ok(event) = native_rx.recv() => {
                    self.handle_native_event(event).await?;
                }
            }
        }
    }
    
    async fn handle_native_event(&mut self, event: NativeEvent) -> Result<()> {
        match event {
            NativeEvent::PaneOutput { pane_id, data, .. } => {
                // Feed to pattern detection
                self.pattern_engine.process(&data);
                // Feed to screen state tracker
                self.screen_tracker.process_output(pane_id, &data);
            }
            NativeEvent::StateChange { pane_id, is_alt_screen, .. } => {
                // Update pane state directly (no polling needed)
                self.pane_states.update_alt_screen(pane_id, is_alt_screen);
            }
            NativeEvent::UserVarChanged { pane_id, name, value, .. } => {
                // Same handling as IPC user-var events
                self.handle_user_var(pane_id, &name, &value).await?;
            }
            // ... other events
        }
        Ok(())
    }
}

```rust
use prometheus::{Registry, Counter, CounterVec, Gauge, GaugeVec, Histogram, HistogramVec};

lazy_static\! {
    pub static ref REGISTRY: Registry = Registry::new();
    
    // Counters
    pub static ref EVENTS_DETECTED: CounterVec = register_counter_vec\!(
        "wa_events_detected_total",
        "Total events detected",
        &["type", "agent"]
    ).unwrap();
    
    pub static ref WORKFLOWS_COMPLETED: CounterVec = register_counter_vec\!(
        "wa_workflows_completed_total",
        "Total workflows completed",
        &["workflow", "status"]
    ).unwrap();
    
    pub static ref POLICY_DECISIONS: CounterVec = register_counter_vec\!(
        "wa_policy_decisions_total",
        "Total policy decisions",
        &["decision", "action"]
    ).unwrap();
    
    // Gauges
    pub static ref PANES_OBSERVED: Gauge = register_gauge\!(
        "wa_panes_observed",
        "Number of observed panes"
    ).unwrap();
    
    pub static ref QUEUE_DEPTH: Gauge = register_gauge\!(
        "wa_queue_depth",
        "Current event queue depth"
    ).unwrap();
    
    // Histograms
    pub static ref PATTERN_MATCH_DURATION: Histogram = register_histogram\!(
        "wa_pattern_match_duration_seconds",
        "Time to run pattern matching",
        vec\![0.0001, 0.0005, 0.001, 0.005, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1]
    ).unwrap();
}
```

### HTTP Endpoint
```rust
use axum::{Router, routing::get};
use prometheus::Encoder;

async fn metrics_handler() -> impl IntoResponse {
    let encoder = prometheus::TextEncoder::new();
    let metric_families = REGISTRY.gather();
    let mut buffer = Vec::new();
    encoder.encode(&metric_families, &mut buffer).unwrap();
    (
        [(header::CONTENT_TYPE, "text/plain; charset=utf-8")],
        buffer,
    )
}

pub fn metrics_router() -> Router {
    Router::new().route("/metrics", get(metrics_handler))
}
```

-{"id":"wa-37x","title":"[EPIC] Reliability Hardening: Circuit Breakers, Chaos Testing, Graceful Degradation","description":"# [EPIC] Reliability Hardening

## Mission
Make wa **resilient to failures** at every level—external services, internal components, and edge cases.

## Why This Matters
wa controls terminal interactions. Failures must be handled gracefully:
- Browser automation fails → do not spam retries
- WezTerm CLI hangs → do not block forever
- DB corrupts → continue observing, degrade gracefully

Reliability is a trust multiplier. Users need confidence that wa will not make things worse when something goes wrong.

## Components

### 1. Circuit Breaker Pattern
Stop hammering failed services:
```
Service: browser_auth
Status: OPEN (tripped 3 failures)
Last failure: 2026-01-18T14:32:01 (timeout)
Retry after: 2026-01-18T14:37:01 (5 min cooldown)
```

Circuit breakers for:
- Browser automation (auth flows)
- `caut` CLI wrapper
- WezTerm CLI (if consistently failing)
- External webhooks

### 2. Watchdog/Heartbeat System
Monitor wa's own health:
- Heartbeat thread checks main loop liveness
- Deadlock detection (no progress for N seconds)
- Auto-restart on unrecoverable hang
- Crash report with diagnostic context

### 3. Chaos Testing Harness
Inject failures to prove resilience:
- Kill WezTerm mid-capture
- Corrupt DB file
- Simulate network timeouts
- Exhaust disk space
- Fill queues to capacity

Chaos tests validate:
- Data integrity after failures
- Graceful degradation behavior
- Recovery procedures work

### 4. Retry with Exponential Backoff
Standardized retry policy:
- Initial: 100ms
- Max: 30s
- Backoff factor: 2x
- Jitter: ±10%
- Max attempts: configurable

Applied to:
- WezTerm CLI calls
- DB writes (transient lock conflicts)
- External service calls

### 5. Graceful Degradation Modes
When components fail, continue with reduced functionality:
- DB read-only → continue observing, queue writes
- Pattern engine error → skip detection, keep ingesting
- Workflow failure → pause workflow, keep observing
- WezTerm CLI unavailable → retry with backoff, surface status

## Observability
All reliability mechanisms emit structured logs:
- Circuit state changes
- Retry attempts and outcomes
- Degradation mode transitions
- Watchdog warnings

## Testing
- Unit tests: Circuit breaker state machine
- Integration tests: Retry behavior under failure injection
- Chaos tests: System survives failure scenarios
- Load tests: Behavior under resource exhaustion

## Success Criteria
- Circuit breakers prevent retry storms
- Watchdog detects and recovers from hangs
- Chaos tests pass for critical failure scenarios
- Graceful degradation maintains core observability

## Acceptance Criteria
- Circuit breakers, retries, and watchdogs prevent runaway failures.
- Graceful degradation keeps core commands usable.
- Chaos harness runs without crashes and produces actionable logs.

 {"id":"wa-37x.1","title":"Circuit breaker infrastructure: state machine, cooldowns, and status reporting","description":"
# Circuit Breaker Infrastructure

## Purpose
Implement a generic circuit breaker pattern to prevent retry storms when services fail.

## State Machine
```
     failures < threshold
CLOSED ◄────────────────────────► OPEN
  │                                  │
  │ failure >= threshold             │ cooldown elapsed
  └──────────────────►               ▼
                              HALF-OPEN
                                  │
                    success → CLOSED
                    failure → OPEN
```

## Implementation
```rust
pub struct CircuitBreaker {
    name: String,
    state: CircuitState,
    failure_count: u32,
    failure_threshold: u32,
    cooldown: Duration,
    last_failure: Option<Instant>,
}

impl CircuitBreaker {
    pub fn call<T>(&self, f: impl FnOnce() -> Result<T>) -> Result<T, CircuitError>;
    pub fn record_success(&mut self);
    pub fn record_failure(&mut self, error: &Error);
    pub fn status(&self) -> CircuitStatus;
}
```

## Circuit Breakers to Implement
- browser_auth: For Playwright auth flows
- caut_cli: For account usage queries
- wezterm_cli: For pane operations
- webhook: For notification delivery

## Status Reporting
```
$ wa doctor --circuits

Circuit Breaker Status:
  browser_auth: CLOSED (healthy)
  caut_cli:     OPEN (3 failures, retry in 4m32s)
  wezterm_cli:  CLOSED (healthy)
  webhook:      HALF-OPEN (testing...)
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] CircuitBreaker struct with state machine
- [ ] Configurable thresholds and cooldowns
- [ ] Status visible in wa doctor
- [ ] Structured logging on state changes

## Testing
- Unit tests for breaker/backoff logic and state transitions.
- Integration tests simulating IO failures and recovery paths.
- Chaos harness scenarios with verbose logs and recovery assertions.

 {"id":"wa-37x.2","title":"Watchdog/heartbeat system: deadlock detection and auto-recovery","description":"
# Watchdog/Heartbeat System

## Purpose
Monitor wa's own health and recover from hangs or deadlocks.

## How It Works
1. Main loop updates heartbeat timestamp each iteration
2. Watchdog thread checks heartbeat every N seconds
3. If heartbeat stale > threshold, trigger recovery

## Implementation
```rust
pub struct Watchdog {
    heartbeat: Arc<AtomicU64>,  // Unix timestamp
    stale_threshold: Duration,
    recovery_action: RecoveryAction,
}

pub enum RecoveryAction {
    LogWarning,
    DumpDiagnostics,
    ForceRestart,
}
```

## Recovery Sequence
1. Log warning with stack traces
2. Dump diagnostic state (queue depths, lock holders)
3. If still stale after N seconds: force restart
4. Generate crash report with diagnostics

## Diagnostic Dump
When watchdog triggers:
- Thread states
- Queue depths
- Lock holder info (if available)
- Recent event log
- Memory usage

## Status Reporting
```
$ wa status --health

Watchdog:
  Main loop: healthy (last heartbeat 0.1s ago)
  DB writer: healthy (queue: 3 items)
  Ingest: healthy (lag: 15ms)
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Heartbeat updated each main loop iteration
- [ ] Watchdog thread monitors heartbeat
- [ ] Recovery actions trigger at configurable threshold
- [ ] Diagnostic dump provides useful context
- [ ] Crash report generated on force restart

## Testing
- Unit tests for breaker/backoff logic and state transitions.
- Integration tests simulating IO failures and recovery paths.
- Chaos harness scenarios with verbose logs and recovery assertions.

 {"id":"wa-37x.3","title":"Chaos testing harness: fault injection framework for resilience validation","description":"
# Chaos Testing Harness

## Purpose
Inject failures systematically to validate wa's resilience under adverse conditions.

## Fault Injection Points
```rust
pub trait FaultInjector {
    fn maybe_fail(&self, point: FaultPoint) -> Result<()>;
}

pub enum FaultPoint {
    WezTermCliCall,
    DbWrite,
    DbRead,
    BrowserAction,
    NetworkRequest,
    FileSystemOp,
}
```

## Test Scenarios

### 1. WezTerm Unavailable
- Kill WezTerm during capture
- Verify: wa degrades gracefully, resumes when available

### 2. DB Corruption
- Corrupt SQLite file mid-write
- Verify: wa detects corruption, reports error, doesn't crash

### 3. Network Timeouts
- Inject timeout on webhook calls
- Verify: circuit breaker trips, notifications queue

### 4. Resource Exhaustion
- Fill disk to capacity
- Verify: wa stops writing, logs error, continues observing

### 5. Queue Overflow
- Fill event queue beyond capacity
- Verify: backpressure applied, no data loss

## Harness Design
```rust
pub struct ChaosHarness {
    injectors: HashMap<FaultPoint, Box<dyn FaultInjector>>,
    scenario: ChaosScenario,
}

pub struct ChaosScenario {
    faults: Vec<ScheduledFault>,
    duration: Duration,
    assertions: Vec<ChaosAssertion>,
}
```

## CI Integration
- Chaos tests run in isolated environment
- Failures generate detailed reports
- Key scenarios gated on merge

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Fault injection framework implemented
- [ ] At least 5 chaos scenarios defined
- [ ] All scenarios pass (system recovers)
- [ ] Chaos tests run in CI (nightly)

## Testing
- Unit tests for breaker/backoff logic and state transitions.
- Integration tests simulating IO failures and recovery paths.
- Chaos harness scenarios with verbose logs and recovery assertions.

# Retry with Exponential Backoff

## Purpose
Provide a consistent, well-tuned retry mechanism for all fallible operations.

## Retry Policy
```rust
pub struct RetryPolicy {
    pub initial_delay: Duration,    // 100ms default
    pub max_delay: Duration,        // 30s default
    pub backoff_factor: f64,        // 2.0 default
    pub jitter_percent: f64,        // 10% default
    pub max_attempts: Option<u32>,  // None = infinite
}

pub async fn with_retry<T>(
    policy: &RetryPolicy,
    operation: impl AsyncFn() -> Result<T>,
) -> Result<T>;
```

## Jitter Formula
```
delay = base_delay * jitter_factor
jitter_factor = 1.0 + random(-jitter_percent, +jitter_percent)
```

## Application Points
- WezTerm CLI calls: 3 attempts, 100ms initial
- DB writes: 5 attempts, 50ms initial
- Webhook delivery: 5 attempts, 1s initial
- Browser actions: 2 attempts, 500ms initial

## Logging
Each retry logged with:
- Attempt number
- Delay applied
- Error that triggered retry

## Integration with Circuit Breaker
- Retries happen within circuit breaker
- Exceeded retries count as circuit failure
- Circuit OPEN prevents retry attempts

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] RetryPolicy struct with configurable parameters
- [ ] with_retry helper function
- [ ] Jitter implemented correctly
- [ ] All I/O points use retry policy
- [ ] Logging shows retry attempts

## Testing
- Unit tests for breaker/backoff logic and state transitions.
- Integration tests simulating IO failures and recovery paths.
- Chaos harness scenarios with verbose logs and recovery assertions.

 {"id":"wa-37x.5","title":"Graceful degradation modes: continue operating with reduced functionality","description":"
# Graceful Degradation Modes

## Purpose
When components fail, continue operating with reduced functionality rather than crashing.

## Degradation Scenarios

### 1. DB Read-Only Mode
**Trigger**: DB write failures (corruption, disk full)
**Behavior**:
- Continue observing panes
- Queue writes in memory (bounded)
- Log warning about degraded mode
- Resume normal operation when DB recovers

### 2. Pattern Engine Failure
**Trigger**: Pattern compilation error, regex timeout
**Behavior**:
- Skip pattern detection
- Continue ingesting output
- Log which patterns are disabled
- Partial detection if some patterns work

### 3. Workflow Engine Pause
**Trigger**: Workflow step fails repeatedly
**Behavior**:
- Pause the failing workflow
- Continue other workflows
- Keep observing for new events
- Notify user of paused workflow

### 4. WezTerm Unavailable
**Trigger**: CLI consistently fails
**Behavior**:
- Stop action attempts
- Continue any cached observation
- Poll for WezTerm availability
- Resume automatically when available

## Status Reporting
```
$ wa status

System Status: DEGRADED

Active Degradations:
  ⚠ DB writes queued (disk full) - 47 pending
  ⚠ Workflow paused: handle_usage_limits (step 3 failed)

Core Functions:
  ✓ Observation: active
  ✓ Detection: active
  ✗ Actions: degraded
```

## Recovery
- Automatic: Poll for recovery conditions
- Manual: `wa recover <component>` to retry

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Each degradation mode implemented
- [ ] Status reports degraded state clearly
- [ ] Automatic recovery when conditions clear
- [ ] Manual recovery command works
- [ ] Core observation continues in all modes

## Testing
- Unit tests for breaker/backoff logic and state transitions.
- Integration tests simulating IO failures and recovery paths.
- Chaos harness scenarios with verbose logs and recovery assertions.

 {"id":"wa-3g9","title":"[EPIC] Performance Optimizations: Lazy Loading, Caching, Pooling","description":"# [EPIC] Performance Optimizations

## Mission
Make wa **fast and efficient** without sacrificing safety or correctness.

## Why This Matters
wa runs continuously in the background. Performance impacts:
- CPU usage (battery life on laptops)
- Memory footprint
- Latency of operations
- Scalability to many panes

Optimizations must be:
- Measurable (benchmarks prove value)
- Safe (no correctness regressions)
- Conditional (only apply when beneficial)

## Components

### 1. Lazy Pattern Compilation
Do not compile regex/Aho-Corasick engines until first use:
```rust
static PATTERN_ENGINE: Lazy<PatternEngine> = Lazy::new(|| {
    PatternEngine::compile(load_packs())
});
```

Benefits:
- Faster startup time
- Memory saved if patterns not used
- Hot-reload ready

### 2. Bloom Filter Pre-Filter
Before running Aho-Corasick, check Bloom filter:
```rust
if !bloom_filter.possibly_contains(&text) {
    return vec![]; // Quick reject
}
// Only then run full pattern matching
```

Budget: < 1µs for Bloom check

### 3. Connection Pooling for WezTerm CLI
Keep warm connections to WezTerm mux server:
```rust
struct WezTermPool {
    connections: Vec<MuxConnection>,
    max_size: usize,
}
```

Benefits:
- Avoid process spawn overhead
- Reduce socket churn
- Enable connection reuse

Note: Only beneficial if vendored mode or mux protocol direct access.

### 4. Memory-Efficient Output Cache
Already exists as `wa-4vx.4.12` - enhance with:
- LRU eviction by memory pressure
- Rolling hash for dedup detection
- Configurable cache size limits

### 5. Background Segment Compression
Already gated by `wa-4vx.3.11` decision - if needed:
- Compress segments older than N days
- Use zstd for good ratio and speed
- Transparent decompression on read
- Background compaction thread

### 6. Incremental FTS Sync
Ensure FTS index only processes new content:
- Track last-indexed segment per pane
- Batch index updates
- Avoid full reindex on restart

## Benchmarks
All optimizations must be validated by Criterion benchmarks:
- Baseline before optimization
- Result after optimization
- Regression tests to prevent backsliding

## Testing
- Benchmark suite for each optimization
- Memory profiling under sustained load
- No correctness regressions (same outputs)

## Success Criteria
- Startup time < 100ms (no feature flags)
- Pattern quick-reject < 1µs for non-matching text
- Memory usage < 100MB for typical workload
- CPU usage < 5% when idle

## Acceptance Criteria
- Each optimization yields measurable latency or memory improvement.
- Performance budgets are enforced in CI.
- Regression thresholds and baselines are documented.

 {"id":"wa-3g9.1","title":"Lazy pattern compilation: defer engine initialization until first use","description":"
# Lazy Pattern Compilation

## Purpose
Defer compilation of pattern matching engines until they're actually needed, improving startup time.

## Current Behavior
PatternEngine compiles all patterns at startup, even if pattern matching isn't used in this run.

## Proposed Behavior
```rust
use once_cell::sync::Lazy;

static PATTERN_ENGINE: Lazy<PatternEngine> = Lazy::new(|| {
    tracing::debug\!("Compiling pattern engine (first use)");
    PatternEngine::compile(load_packs())
});
```

## Benefits
- Faster startup for non-watch commands
- Memory saved if patterns not used
- Enables future hot-reload capability

## Benchmark Targets
- Startup time: reduce by ~50-100ms (depends on pattern count)
- First pattern match: may have one-time ~100ms delay
- Overall: net positive for typical usage

## Implementation Notes
- Use `once_cell::sync::Lazy` for thread-safe lazy init
- Ensure compile errors surface correctly
- Consider warming on `wa watch` start

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Pattern engine compiled lazily
- [ ] Commands that don't use patterns are faster
- [ ] First pattern match latency acceptable
- [ ] Benchmark proves improvement

## Testing
- Criterion benchmarks with budgets and baseline comparison.
- Microbenchmarks for the specific optimization.
- CI regression guard with perf logs.
